home_hero_desc: A visual, interactive guide to how Large Language Models work — from raw text to next-token probabilities. No backend, no API calls; everything runs in your browser.
home_topic_tokenization_desc: How raw text is split into tokens using algorithms like BPE and WordPiece.
home_topic_embeddings_desc: Turning tokens into dense vectors and navigating semantic space.
home_topic_transformer_desc: Encoder/decoder blocks, residual connections, and layer normalisation.
home_topic_attention_desc: Self-attention, multi-head attention, and scaled dot-product attention.
home_topic_training_desc: Pre-training objectives like next-token prediction and masked language modelling.
home_topic_inference_desc: Temperature, top-k, top-p, and greedy decoding strategies.
home_topic_scaling_desc: Chinchilla and compute-optimal training — what happens when you scale up.
home_topic_finetuning_desc: Supervised fine-tuning, reward models, and proximal policy optimisation.
tok_intro: Before a language model can process text, the text must be converted into a sequence of discrete tokens. A token is the basic unit the model operates on — it can be a word, a sub-word, a single character, or even whitespace.
tok_subword_p: Early NLP models used whole-word vocabularies, but these struggle with rare or unseen words. Character-level models handle any word but produce very long sequences. Sub-word tokenization (e.g. BPE, WordPiece, Unigram) finds a middle ground: common words get their own token, while rare words are split into familiar pieces.
tok_bpe_p: BPE starts with a character-level vocabulary and iteratively merges the most frequent pair of adjacent tokens until the vocabulary reaches a target size. GPT models (including GPT-4) use a variant called tiktoken.
tok_bpe_li1: Initialise vocabulary with individual characters (and a special end-of-word symbol).
tok_bpe_li2: Count all adjacent token pairs in the training corpus.
tok_bpe_li3: Merge the most frequent pair into a new token.
tok_bpe_li4: Repeat until the vocabulary is large enough.
tok_wordpiece_p: Used by BERT and its derivatives. Similar to BPE but merges pairs that maximise the likelihood of the training data rather than raw frequency. Sub-word tokens that don't start a word are prefixed with ## (e.g., playing → play + ##ing).
tok_practice_p: Numbers, punctuation, and non-English scripts often tokenize inefficiently — a single Chinese character may map to multiple tokens. This is why token count (not character count) matters when estimating model costs and context window usage.
tok_intermediate_p: At this point, the core idea should be clear: tokenization is a compression and representation strategy. Strong tokenization keeps common patterns compact, handles rare forms gracefully, and directly affects how much useful context fits into a model call.
tok_h2_checkpoint: Checkpoint: What You Should Understand Now
tok_checkpoint_intro: Before moving on, verify that you can explain:
tok_checkpoint_li1: Why modern LLMs prefer sub-word tokenization over whole-word or pure character vocabularies.
tok_checkpoint_li2: How tokenizer design changes context usage, latency, and cost in real applications.
tok_checkpoint_li3: Why the same sentence can produce different token counts across model families.
emb_intro: Tokens are discrete symbols — they have no inherent numerical meaning. An embedding is a learned dense vector that gives each token a position in a continuous high-dimensional space. Similar tokens end up near each other.
emb_onehot_p: A vocabulary of 50,000 words could be represented with one-hot vectors of length 50,000, but these are sparse and encode no similarity. An embedding matrix E ∈ ℝ^(vocab × d_model) maps each token ID to a dense vector of dimension d_model (e.g., 768 for BERT-base, 4096 for LLaMA-2-7B).
emb_examples_intro: A useful way to think about embeddings is: words used in similar contexts get similar vectors. That gives us practical behavior like:
emb_example_synonyms: Synonyms (car, automobile) tend to be close in space.
emb_example_analogy: Relational patterns (Paris→France, Tokyo→Japan) often become consistent geometric directions.
emb_example_domain: Domain terms cluster together (doctor, nurse, clinic) and separate from unrelated groups (mountain, river, forest).
emb_example_composition: Phrase meaning can emerge from nearby token vectors, helping models represent short expressions like "credit card" vs "river bank".
emb_semantic_p: Embeddings capture rich semantic and syntactic relationships. The classic example:
emb_direction_p: This works because direction in embedding space corresponds to meaning. "Royalty" is a direction; "gender" is another.
emb_positional_p: Token embeddings tell us what each token means, but not where it appears in a sentence. Positional encodings add explicit order information so "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" are represented differently.
emb_positional_attention_bridge: We will explain why this matters for attention in the next chapter:
emb_pos_sinusoidal_desc: (original Transformer): fixed sine/cosine patterns at different frequencies.
emb_pos_learned_desc: (BERT, GPT): a trainable position embedding table.
emb_pos_rope_desc: (LLaMA, Mistral): rotary or relative positional encodings that generalise better to long sequences.
emb_scatter_desc: Pre-computed 2-D projections showing how semantically similar words cluster together.
emb_learned_p: The embedding matrix is not hand-crafted — it starts as random numbers and is trained jointly with the rest of the model. During training, every time a token participates in a prediction, gradients flow back into its row of the embedding matrix and nudge it in a direction that lowers the loss. After billions of tokens, words that play similar roles end up with similar vectors simply because moving them closer made the model's predictions better.
emb_learned_step_lookup: Look up the d-dimensional vector for each input token ID from the embedding table.
emb_learned_step_forward: Feed those vectors through the Transformer to produce a prediction.
emb_learned_step_loss: Compare the prediction to the target and compute a loss.
emb_learned_step_update: Backpropagate the loss; the optimizer updates the embedding rows for the tokens that were used.
emb_worked_intro: To build intuition, imagine an embedding of just three dimensions where each axis loosely captures a concept like "royal", "feminine", and "animal". Real models use hundreds or thousands of dimensions and the axes are not human-interpretable, but the geometry behaves similarly:
emb_worked_caption: Toy 3-D embeddings — illustration only, not from a trained model.
emb_worked_followup: With these toy vectors, king − man + woman ≈ (0.9, 0.85, 0.05), which is very close to queen. The same arithmetic that worked on the classic example works here because the "royal" and "feminine" directions are aligned with real axes.
emb_similarity_intro: Once tokens live in a vector space, we can quantify how related they are. The most common measure in NLP is cosine similarity, which looks at the angle between two vectors and ignores their magnitude:
emb_similarity_values: Values range from −1 (opposite meanings) through 0 (unrelated) to +1 (essentially the same). Embedding-based search engines, retrieval-augmented generation, and clustering all rely on this score.
emb_similarity_examples_intro: Typical cosine similarities you might see with a well-trained model:
emb_h2_lifecycle: Where Embeddings Live in an LLM
emb_lifecycle_p: Every modern LLM has at least three places where embedding-like vectors appear, and it helps to keep them straight:
emb_life_input_desc: the lookup table that turns each input token ID into the first vector the model sees.
emb_life_hidden_desc: the per-token vectors produced by every Transformer layer. These are richer, context-aware embeddings.
emb_life_output_desc: a final matrix (often tied to the input embeddings) that maps the last hidden state back to vocabulary logits used for prediction.
emb_pitfalls_intro: A few things to watch out for when working with embeddings:
emb_pitfall_bias: Embeddings absorb the biases of their training data — gender, regional, and cultural associations can become geometric directions.
emb_pitfall_polysemy: A single vector has to cover every sense of a word (bank → river vs. money). Contextual embeddings from hidden states address this; static lookups do not.
emb_pitfall_oov: Out-of-vocabulary tokens fall back to sub-word pieces, so unfamiliar names or jargon may have weaker embeddings until the model is fine-tuned.
tr_intro: The Transformer, introduced in "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017), replaced recurrent networks with pure attention, enabling massive parallelisation during training.
tr_enc_dec_intro: The original Transformer has two stacks:
tr_enc_desc: reads the full input sequence bidirectionally. Used in models like BERT for understanding tasks.
tr_dec_desc: generates tokens auto-regressively, attending only to previous positions (causal masking). Used in GPT-family models.
tr_encdec_desc: used in T5, BART, and the original machine translation model. The decoder attends to encoder outputs via cross-attention.
tr_block_intro: Every block in the stack follows the same pattern:
tr_block_mhsa_desc: each position attends to all others (or just previous ones in the decoder).
tr_block_residual_desc: the attention output is added to the block's input, then normalised.
tr_block_ffn_desc: two linear layers with a non-linearity (ReLU or GELU) applied position-wise.
tr_block_residual2_desc: again, wrap the FFN output with a skip connection and normalisation.
tr_residual_p: Residual (skip) connections allow gradients to flow directly through the network, preventing vanishing gradients in deep stacks. They also let the model learn an incremental update to the representation rather than a full transformation.
tr_layernorm_p: Unlike batch norm, layer norm operates over the feature dimension independently for each token. This makes it insensitive to batch size and well-suited to variable-length sequences. Modern models (GPT-2+) typically use pre-norm (normalise before the sub-layer) rather than the original post-norm, improving training stability.
tr_gpt2_desc: 12 layers, d_model = 768, 12 attention heads, 117 M params
tr_why_p: Before 2017, language models were built on recurrent networks (RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs) that processed tokens one at a time. That sequential dependency made training slow and made it hard to relate words that were far apart in a sentence. Transformers solved both problems at once:
tr_why_parallel_desc: every position is processed at the same time, so training scales with GPU/TPU throughput instead of sentence length.
tr_why_longrange_desc: self-attention gives every token direct access to every other token in one step, regardless of distance.
tr_why_transfer_desc: the same architecture pre-trained on raw text generalises across summarisation, translation, chat, code, and more.
tr_h2_walkthrough: Worked Example: A Token's Journey Through a Block
tr_walkthrough_intro: Imagine the input sentence "The cat sat on the mat" has been tokenised and embedded. Following the vector for the token "sat" through a single Transformer block makes the architecture concrete:
tr_walk_step1_desc: "sat" arrives as a d_model-dimensional vector that already contains its embedding and positional information.
tr_walk_step2_desc: the block produces Q, K, V projections for every token and computes attention weights. The token "sat" pays strong attention to its subject "cat" and its prepositional phrase "on the mat", pulling those vectors into a context-aware update.
tr_walk_step3_desc: the attention output is added back to the original "sat" vector and normalised, so the block only has to learn an incremental refinement.
tr_walk_step4_desc: two linear layers with a GELU non-linearity mix features within the token, sharpening abstractions like "this is a past-tense action verb".
tr_walk_step5_desc: the FFN output is added and normalised, producing the block's final representation of "sat" that is fed to the next block.
tr_walk_followup: Stack this same process 12, 32, or 96 times and the per-token vectors become rich enough to predict the next token, classify the sentence, or translate it.
tr_choose_intro: Not every task needs every part of the original Transformer. A quick guide to picking a variant:
tr_choose_encoder_desc: best for understanding tasks like classification, named-entity recognition, and embedding generation, where you need to see the whole input at once.
tr_choose_decoder_desc: best for generation: chat, code completion, story writing. Causal masking enforces left-to-right prediction.
tr_choose_encdec_desc: best for sequence-to-sequence tasks where input and output are different texts: translation, summarisation, question answering.
tr_variants_intro: The 2017 recipe is still recognisable in today's models, but production systems sprinkle in optimisations:
tr_variant_prenorm: Pre-norm instead of post-norm for more stable training in very deep stacks.
tr_variant_rope: Rotary or ALiBi positional encodings for better extrapolation to long contexts.
tr_variant_gqa: Grouped-query / multi-query attention to shrink the KV cache during inference.
tr_variant_swiglu: SwiGLU or GeGLU activations in the FFN, which empirically outperform plain GELU.
tr_variant_moe: Mixture-of-Experts FFN layers (e.g., Mixtral, Switch Transformer) that activate only a few "experts" per token to scale parameters without scaling compute.
att_intro: Attention is the core operation that allows the Transformer to model relationships between tokens regardless of their distance in the sequence. Unlike RNNs, there's no sequential bottleneck.
att_sdp_intro: Given an input matrix X, three projections are computed:
att_q_desc: what this position is looking for.
att_k_desc: what each position has to offer.
att_scaling_p: Dividing by √d_k (the key dimension) prevents the dot products from growing large and pushing softmax into saturation.
att_mha_p: Instead of a single attention function, the model runs h attention heads in parallel, each with its own Q/K/V projections into a smaller subspace (dimension d_k = d_model / h). The outputs are concatenated and projected back:
att_mha_p2: Different heads can specialise — one might focus on syntactic agreement, another on co-reference, another on semantic similarity.
att_causal_p: In decoder-only models (GPT family), the attention matrix is masked so each position can only attend to itself and earlier positions. This preserves the auto-regressive property: the model predicts token t using only tokens 1 … t−1.
att_flash_p: The naive attention implementation materialises the full N × N attention matrix, which is O(N²) in memory. Flash Attention rewrites the computation using tiling to keep it in fast SRAM, reducing memory to O(N) while computing exactly the same result.
train_intro: Training a large language model involves two broad phases: massive pre-training on unlabelled text, and optional fine-tuning for specific tasks or alignment.
train_clm_p: Used by GPT-family models. The model predicts the next token given all previous tokens. The loss is the average negative log-likelihood across all positions:
train_clm_p2: Because every position provides a training signal, CLM is extremely data-efficient — a single document of length T gives T training examples.
train_mlm_p: Used by BERT. Approximately 15% of tokens are replaced with a [MASK] token, and the model predicts the original. This allows bidirectional context but the model can't be used auto-regressively out of the box.
train_span_p: Random spans of text are replaced with sentinel tokens. The decoder must reconstruct the missing spans, giving a sequence-to-sequence pre-training signal.
train_loss_p: LLM training minimises cross-entropy loss using mini-batch stochastic gradient descent. Because the loss landscape has many local minima, several tricks help:
train_adam_desc: adaptive learning rates per parameter, with weight decay decoupled.
train_lr_desc: ramp up slowly, then decay smoothly to near-zero.
train_clip_desc: cap gradient norm to prevent explosive updates.
train_mixed_desc: halves memory and speeds up matrix multiplications on modern GPUs.
train_scale_p: Modern frontier models are trained on trillions of tokens. GPT-3 was trained on ~300B tokens; LLaMA-2 on 2T tokens. Training compute is measured in FLOPs — a 7B-parameter model trained on 1T tokens uses roughly 6 × 7×10⁹ × 10¹² ≈ 4×10²² FLOPs.
train_data_intro: Raw text from the web, books, code, and other sources is:
train_data_li3: Tokenized and packed into fixed-length chunks (e.g., 2048 or 8192 tokens).
train_data_li4: Shuffled and streamed from storage during training.
inf_intro: After training, the model produces a logit vector over the vocabulary at each step. How we convert those logits into a sampled token determines the model's behaviour.
inf_greedy_p: Always pick the token with the highest probability. Fast and deterministic, but can get stuck in repetitive loops and misses the global optimal sequence.
inf_beam_p: Keep the top-k candidate sequences at each step, expanding all simultaneously. More expensive but finds a higher overall probability sequence. Common in machine translation; less common in open-ended generation.
inf_temp_p: Before softmax, divide the logits by a temperature T:
inf_temp_li3_desc: distribution flattens → more random, creative, but also more likely to be incoherent.
inf_topk_p: Keep only the k highest-probability tokens and renormalise. Cuts off the long tail of unlikely tokens. Typical values: k = 40–200.
inf_topp_p: Keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p (e.g., p = 0.9). Unlike top-k, the number of candidates adapts to the distribution's entropy — sharp distributions keep fewer candidates, flat ones keep more.
inf_rep_p: Divide logits of already-generated tokens by a penalty factor > 1. Reduces loops without fully eliminating repeat content.
scl_intro: Empirical scaling laws describe how model performance improves predictably as we increase model size, dataset size, and compute budget.
scl_h2_kaplan: Kaplan et al. (2020) — OpenAI Scaling Laws
scl_kaplan_p: The original scaling-laws paper found that language model loss follows a power law with respect to:
scl_kaplan_p2: Crucially, these are independent — each can be scaled without running out of the others' benefit. The paper suggested training very large models on relatively small data budgets.
scl_chinchilla_p: DeepMind's re-analysis showed that Kaplan et al. had underestimated the value of data. Their finding:
scl_chinchilla_quote: For compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale equally. The optimal token count is approximately 20× the parameter count.
scl_chinchilla_p2: GPT-3 (175B params, 300B tokens) was significantly under-trained by this metric. The Chinchilla model (70B params, 1.4T tokens) outperformed GPT-3 despite using less compute.
scl_impl_li1: Models trained since 2023 (LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon) mostly follow Chinchilla ratios or even exceed them (LLaMA-2: 7B params on 2T tokens = ~286× the parameter count).
scl_impl_li2: Inference efficiency matters: a smaller, well-trained model is cheaper to serve than a large, under-trained one with the same loss.
scl_impl_li3: Data quality matters as much as quantity — "textbook quality" data (phi-1, phi-2) can beat much larger corpora.
scl_emergent_p: Some capabilities (chain-of-thought reasoning, arithmetic, multi-step logic) appear suddenly as models cross certain parameter thresholds. This "emergence" is not fully explained by smooth power-law scaling and is an active research area.
scl_downstream_p: Pre-training loss is a good proxy but not the only metric. Some tasks (coding, mathematics) benefit disproportionately from scale, while others plateau earlier. Benchmark-specific scaling curves can differ significantly from perplexity curves.
ft_intro: A pre-trained model is a powerful next-token predictor, but it doesn't know how to follow instructions or behave helpfully and safely. Fine-tuning bridges that gap.
ft_sft_p: The first step is to fine-tune the model on a dataset of (prompt, ideal response) pairs written by human annotators. This teaches the model to follow the instruction format and produce coherent, helpful responses.
ft_sft_p2: The loss is the same causal language modelling loss — the model is trained to predict the response tokens given the prompt. The prompt tokens are often masked so their loss doesn't contribute.
ft_reward_p: Annotators rank multiple model responses to the same prompt. A reward model (RM) is trained on these pairwise preferences using a ranking loss:
ft_reward_p2: where y_w is the preferred response and y_l is the less preferred one. The reward model learns to assign higher scalar scores to better responses.
ft_rlhf_p: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) uses the reward model as an environment and optimises the SFT model (now the policy) using Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO):
ft_rlhf_li1: Sample responses from the current policy for a batch of prompts.
ft_rlhf_li2: Score each response with the reward model.
ft_rlhf_li3: Update the policy to maximise reward while staying close to the SFT model (via a KL penalty).
ft_rlhf_p2: The KL penalty prevents the policy from drifting too far from the SFT baseline and generating gibberish that tricks the RM.
ft_dpo_p: DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) skips the RL loop entirely. It shows that the reward-maximisation objective can be solved implicitly by directly fine-tuning on pairwise preference data:
ft_dpo_p2: DPO is simpler and more stable than PPO and has become the dominant RLHF alternative in open-source models.
ft_peft_p: Full fine-tuning of a 70B-parameter model is expensive. PEFT methods update only a small fraction of parameters:
ft_lora_desc: adds low-rank update matrices ΔW = BA alongside frozen weights.
ft_qlora_desc: LoRA on a 4-bit quantised base model, enabling 7B fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU.
ft_prefix_desc: prepend learnable soft token embeddings; freeze all model weights.
tok_deep_1_intro: This deep dive compares the three subword families that show up most often in modern NLP systems: BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece.
tok_deep_1_p1: BPE merges frequent adjacent units and is simple to scale. WordPiece optimizes merges for likelihood improvement. SentencePiece can train directly from raw text and supports both BPE and unigram approaches.
tok_deep_1_p2: In practice, they trade off vocabulary compactness, unknown-word handling, and training complexity differently. Understanding those tradeoffs helps explain why different model families choose different tokenizers.
tok_deep_2_title: Tokenization Deep Dive 2 — Real Model Tokenizers
tok_deep_2_intro: Production LLM systems rely on concrete tokenizer implementations such as TikToken vocabularies (r50k, p50k, cl100k) rather than abstract algorithm names.
tok_deep_2_p1: Different vocabularies are optimized for different workloads: plain text, code-heavy data, or multilingual corpora. That is why the same prompt can produce different token counts on different models.
tok_deep_2_p2: For developers, this directly affects context limits, truncation behavior, and API cost. Token budgeting should always be done with the target model's tokenizer.
tok_deep_3_title: Tokenization Deep Dive 3 — Multilingual & Edge Cases
tok_deep_3_intro: Tokenization quality changes significantly across scripts and symbols. CJK text, emojis, punctuation patterns, and whitespace normalization all create edge cases.
tok_deep_3_p1: Two strings that look almost identical to humans can map to very different token sequences because of Unicode composition, hidden separators, or punctuation variants.
tok_deep_3_p2: This matters for product reliability: limits can be exceeded unexpectedly, retrieval chunks can misalign, and prompt templates may behave differently across languages.
tok_deep_4_title: Tokenization Deep Dive 4 — Prompt Engineering Implications
tok_deep_4_intro: Prompt design is not just semantic; it is also token-economic. Phrasing choices influence how much information fits into a fixed context window.
tok_deep_4_p1: Compact wording, predictable formatting, and careful examples can reduce token usage while keeping instruction quality high.
tok_deep_4_p2: For long workflows, token efficiency compounds: lower cost per request, lower latency, and more usable context left for grounding data and conversation history.
sub_tiktoken_intro: TikToken is OpenAI's fast tokenization library for GPT-style models. Instead of splitting text into whole words, it applies a byte-pair encoding vocabulary learned from large corpora, letting common strings stay intact while rare words break into smaller pieces.
sub_tiktoken_p1: A useful detail is that different GPT families use different vocabularies. GPT-2 used r50k_base, later code-heavy models used p50k_base, and modern GPT-4-class systems often use cl100k_base, which handles whitespace, code, and multilingual text more efficiently.
sub_tiktoken_p2: When developers count tokens before calling an API, they are usually reproducing the model's exact TikToken rules. That matters because context limits, pricing, and truncation are all based on tokens rather than characters or words.
sub_wordpiece_intro: WordPiece is the subword algorithm popularised by BERT and DistilBERT. It builds a vocabulary of pieces that can compose common words directly while still representing rare words by stitching together smaller segments.
sub_wordpiece_p1: Unlike plain BPE, WordPiece chooses merges that best improve the likelihood of the training corpus. In BERT tokenizers, continuing pieces are marked with ##, so a word like unhappiness may become un, ##happy, ##ness.
sub_wordpiece_p2: This balance makes WordPiece robust for open-vocabulary language understanding. The encoder sees frequent whole words as compact tokens, while rare names or domain terms can still be represented without falling back to characters only.
sub_sentencepiece_intro: SentencePiece is a tokenizer toolkit that treats raw text as a stream of Unicode characters, including spaces. That means it can train directly from text without a separate rule-based pre-tokenization step.
sub_sentencepiece_p1: It supports both BPE and unigram language-model tokenization. The unigram variant starts with many candidate pieces and removes the ones that contribute least to the likelihood of the corpus, leaving a compact probabilistic vocabulary.
sub_sentencepiece_p2: Models such as T5 and many LLaMA-family checkpoints use SentencePiece because it works well across languages and scripts. Its explicit handling of whitespace also makes detokenization reliable and reversible.
sub_word2vec_intro: Word2Vec is an early neural embedding method that learns dense vectors from local context windows. It showed that simple predictive tasks can produce semantic spaces where related words cluster together.
sub_word2vec_p1: CBOW predicts the centre word from surrounding context, while Skip-Gram does the reverse and predicts nearby words from the centre token. Skip-Gram is often better for rare words because each occurrence generates multiple training signals.
sub_word2vec_p2: Although modern LLM embeddings are contextual rather than static, Word2Vec remains a useful mental model. It introduced the idea that meaning can be encoded geometrically, which later influenced transformer embedding layers.
sub_positional_intro: Self-attention alone has no notion of order, so transformers need an extra signal that tells the model which token came first, second, or later. Positional encodings inject that sequence structure into the representation.
sub_positional_p1: The original transformer used fixed sinusoidal features whose frequencies vary by dimension. Because nearby positions have related patterns, the model can infer both absolute position and relative distance from those waves.
sub_positional_p2: Modern decoder models often prefer rotary position embeddings, or RoPE. RoPE rotates query and key vectors by position-dependent angles, which preserves relative offsets elegantly and tends to extrapolate better to longer contexts.
sub_encoder_intro: The encoder stack in the original transformer reads the full input bidirectionally. Every layer mixes information across the entire sequence, making it well suited for tasks such as classification, retrieval, and span extraction.
sub_encoder_p1: Each encoder block contains self-attention followed by a position-wise feed-forward network, with residual connections and layer normalization around both parts. Because there is no causal mask, every token can attend to tokens on its left and right.
sub_encoder_p2: BERT-style models keep only the encoder half because they focus on understanding rather than generation. The final hidden states become contextual embeddings that downstream heads can use for many different tasks.
sub_decoder_intro: The decoder stack is built for auto-regressive generation. At step t, it predicts the next token using only tokens 1 through t-1, so its internal attention must respect left-to-right causality.
sub_decoder_p1: A causal mask blocks attention to future positions by setting those logits to negative infinity before the softmax. In encoder-decoder models, the decoder also includes cross-attention so it can condition on encoded source tokens.
sub_decoder_p2: GPT-style LLMs keep just the decoder stack because next-token prediction is enough to learn broad language competence. During inference, the same block is reused repeatedly as the generated sequence grows.
sub_ffn_intro: Inside every transformer block, the feed-forward network processes each token position independently after attention has mixed information across positions. It is where much of the model's non-linear transformation capacity lives.
sub_ffn_p1: A standard FFN expands the hidden size to a larger intermediate dimension, applies a non-linearity such as ReLU, GELU, or SwiGLU, and then projects back down. The expansion ratio is often around 4x in classic transformer designs.
sub_ffn_p2: You can think of attention as moving information between tokens and the FFN as transforming what each token representation means. Both pieces are necessary: attention alone is too linear, and FFNs alone cannot exchange context across positions.
sub_selfattn_intro: Self-attention lets each token build a weighted summary of other tokens in the same sequence. The weights depend on learned query and key comparisons, so the model can focus on whatever context is useful for the current position.
sub_selfattn_p1: In scaled dot-product attention, each score is a dot product between a query and a key, divided by the square root of the key dimension. The softmax converts those scores into attention probabilities that are used to combine value vectors.
sub_selfattn_p2: This mechanism is powerful because dependency length does not matter much. A token can attend strongly to another token many words away without passing through a long recurrent chain, which is one reason transformers scale so well.
sub_multihead_intro: Multi-head attention runs several smaller attention mechanisms in parallel instead of one large head. Each head gets its own learned projections of queries, keys, and values.
sub_multihead_p1: After each head produces an output, the results are concatenated and multiplied by an output projection matrix. This lets the model combine different views of the same context into one representation of size d_model.
sub_multihead_p2: In practice, different heads often specialise. Some track syntax, some focus on long-range references, and some act more like induction heads that copy repeated patterns. The division into heads encourages that diversity of behavior.
sub_kvcache_intro: A KV-cache stores the key and value tensors produced for previous tokens during decoder inference. Without it, the model would recompute attention states for the entire prefix at every generation step.
sub_kvcache_p1: With caching, only the newest token needs fresh query, key, and value projections. The new query attends over the cached keys and values plus the current token, reducing repeated work and making long generations much faster.
sub_kvcache_p2: The trade-off is memory. As the sequence grows, the cache grows with it across layers and heads, so serving systems must balance latency, context length, and GPU memory usage carefully.
sub_nexttoken_intro: Causal language modelling trains a decoder to predict the next token in a sequence. For every position, the correct label is simply the next observed token from the training text.
sub_nexttoken_p1: Because a document of length T yields almost T supervised targets, the objective is extremely data-efficient. The model learns grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns indirectly by compressing what usually comes next in human text.
sub_nexttoken_p2: This objective also matches deployment. At inference time the model repeatedly predicts one next token, appends it to the context, and repeats, so the training task and generation loop are closely aligned.
sub_maskedlm_intro: Masked language modelling hides selected tokens and asks the model to reconstruct them from both left and right context. BERT popularised this objective for language understanding rather than generation.
sub_maskedlm_p1: Only a subset of positions contribute to the loss, typically around 15 percent. Some selected tokens are replaced with [MASK], some with random tokens, and some are kept unchanged so the model does not overfit to a special placeholder.
sub_maskedlm_p2: Because the encoder can inspect the full sentence, MLM produces strong bidirectional representations. The downside is that the model is not naturally trained for left-to-right text generation the way GPT models are.
sub_loss_intro: Cross-entropy loss measures how much probability the model assigns to the correct next token. If the model is confident and correct, the loss is low; if it spreads probability mass elsewhere, the loss rises.
sub_loss_p1: For language models, perplexity is the exponential of average cross-entropy. It can be interpreted as the model's effective branching factor: lower perplexity means the model is less surprised by the test data.
sub_loss_p2: Researchers like these metrics because they are differentiable and easy to aggregate across huge corpora. Still, lower perplexity does not always guarantee better helpfulness or reasoning, so downstream evaluations remain important.
sub_temperature_intro: Temperature rescales logits before the softmax during sampling. It does not change what the model knows, but it changes how sharply or broadly that knowledge is expressed in the output distribution.
sub_temperature_p1: When temperature is below 1, high-probability tokens become even more dominant and the model behaves more deterministically. When temperature is above 1, the distribution flattens and lower-probability alternatives become more likely.
sub_temperature_p2: This makes temperature a trade-off knob between reliability and variety. Very low values can sound repetitive, while very high values often introduce incoherent or off-topic continuations.
sub_topkp_intro: Top-k and top-p sampling both trim the tail of the token distribution before sampling. They are practical ways to prevent extremely unlikely tokens from being chosen just because temperature added randomness.
sub_topkp_p1: Top-k keeps exactly the k most likely candidates, while top-p keeps the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold p. Top-p adapts to uncertainty, so sharp distributions yield fewer options and flat ones yield more.
sub_topkp_p2: In real systems these methods are often combined with temperature and repetition penalties. The overall behavior depends on the interaction of all these controls, not on any single parameter in isolation.
sub_beamsearch_intro: Beam search keeps several partial hypotheses alive instead of committing to the single best token at each step. It is designed to approximate the highest-probability full sequence rather than a locally greedy choice.
sub_beamsearch_p1: At every decoding step, each beam is expanded by likely next tokens, the combined candidates are scored, and only the top beams survive. Length penalties are often added so the search does not unfairly prefer very short sequences.
sub_beamsearch_p2: This works well for structured tasks such as translation or summarization, where one globally coherent answer is desired. For open-ended chat or creative writing, stochastic sampling often feels more natural than beam search.
sub_chinchilla_intro: The Chinchilla paper argued that many large models were under-trained relative to their size. For a fixed compute budget, performance improves when parameter count and training tokens are scaled together more evenly.
sub_chinchilla_p1: A memorable rule of thumb from the paper is that compute-optimal training uses roughly 20 training tokens per parameter. Under that view, a smaller model trained on much more data can beat a larger model trained on too little text.
sub_chinchilla_p2: This changed model design strategy across the field. It pushed researchers to think of parameters, data, and training compute as a joint budget allocation problem rather than chasing parameter count alone.
sub_emergent_intro: Emergent abilities are behaviors that appear weak or absent in smaller models but become much more visible after scaling. Examples often include arithmetic, chain-of-thought prompting, or tool-like instruction following.
sub_emergent_p1: One interpretation is that capability curves are smooth underneath, but benchmarks make them look sudden because they use hard thresholds such as exact-match accuracy. Another view is that new circuits really do form once models reach a certain scale.
sub_emergent_p2: Either way, emergence matters because it warns us that capability forecasting is not always linear. Small improvements in loss can translate into surprisingly large jumps on selected downstream tasks.
sub_sft_intro: Supervised fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained model using curated prompt and response pairs. The goal is to teach the model the format and behavior expected in instruction-following settings.
sub_sft_p1: The optimization objective is still next-token prediction, but now the targets come from high-quality demonstrations written by humans or filtered systems. This stage often teaches the model to answer directly, stay on task, and follow conversational conventions.
sub_sft_p2: SFT is usually the first alignment step because it is comparatively stable and data-efficient. A strong SFT baseline also gives later preference-optimization methods a much better starting policy.
sub_rlhf_intro: Reinforcement learning from human feedback uses preference data to push a model toward outputs that people judge as more helpful, honest, or harmless. A reward model is trained to predict those preferences from ranked examples.
sub_rlhf_p1: PPO then updates the language model policy to increase reward while staying close to the supervised baseline. The closeness term, usually implemented as a KL penalty, reduces reward hacking and keeps the model from drifting into nonsense.
sub_rlhf_p2: Although newer methods such as DPO are simpler, RLHF with PPO remains historically important. It established the modern alignment pipeline used in systems like InstructGPT and many later chat models.
sub_lora_intro: LoRA is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that freezes the original model weights and learns small low-rank update matrices instead. The key idea is that many useful task-specific changes live in a much lower-dimensional subspace than the full weight matrix.
sub_lora_p1: During training, a weight update is represented as ΔW = BA where B and A have low rank. This drastically cuts the number of trainable parameters and optimizer states, which reduces memory and compute requirements.
sub_lora_p2: Because the base model stays frozen, LoRA adapters are easy to swap, merge, or distribute separately. That is why LoRA and QLoRA became the default way to adapt large open-weight models on modest hardware.
emb_checkpoint_h2: Checkpoint: What you should understand now
emb_checkpoint_intro: Before moving on, make sure you can explain:
emb_checkpoint_li1: How vector geometry supports analogies and semantic neighborhoods.
emb_checkpoint_li2: Why cosine similarity is often preferred over raw dot product.
emb_checkpoint_li3: Why contextual embeddings change with surrounding text.
sub_word2vec_p3: Word2Vec geometry is easiest to understand through vector arithmetic. Offsets such as king - man + woman often land close to queen because the model learns reusable semantic directions.
sub_word2vec_p4: That behavior is not magic; it emerges from many local context predictions that align related words into smooth neighborhoods in vector space.
sub_positional_p3: Sinusoidal encodings give each position a deterministic pattern of sines and cosines at different frequencies, letting attention recover both absolute and relative location cues.
sub_positional_p4: Modern variants like learned positional embeddings and RoPE change how sequence order is injected, often improving long-context behavior in decoder models.
emb_vec_demo_desc: Pick an analogy template and inspect where a − b + c lands in a toy embedding space.
emb_wave_desc: Adjust position and dimension to see how sinusoidal encodings shift.
emb_cosine_intro: Cosine similarity compares vector direction, not magnitude, so it captures semantic alignment even when vectors have different lengths.
emb_cosine_p1: The dot product mixes both angle and length. Cosine similarity normalizes by both magnitudes, yielding a score from -1 to 1 that is easier to interpret.
emb_cosine_p2: In embedding retrieval systems, nearest-neighbor search often uses cosine similarity because it is robust to scale differences.
emb_cosine_demo_desc: Switch between word-pair presets and inspect the angle-based similarity score.
emb_contextual_intro: Static embeddings assign one vector per word type. Contextual embeddings assign one vector per token usage, so meaning shifts with surrounding text.
emb_contextual_p1: This solves polysemy: the token bank in a finance sentence and in a river sentence should not share exactly the same representation.
emb_contextual_p2: Transformer layers gradually inject context, moving token vectors toward sense-specific regions of embedding space.
emb_context_demo_desc: Switch sentence context for a polysemous word and observe how the semantic position shifts.
emb_scatter_hover_hint: Hover a point to inspect its word and cluster, or click points/legend to filter clusters.
